content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

September 12, 1980


Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs & Nielsen
1225 Eighth Street, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Nielsen:

     I have been asked to respond to your letter of March 15,
1980, to Ted Prim, and to Mr. Hodgson's letters of March 10, and
June 16, 1980, to Tom Sobel.  In your March 15 letter, you re-
quested advice regarding reporting obligations under Government
Code Section 84303 for expenditures to companies that produce
radio or television spots.  You described a factual situation in
which an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee
writes a script for such a spot advertisement, and an independent
production company is hired by the agency for a flat fee to
produce the advertisement.

     Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as
payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee
to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement,
or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report
on election trends for a candidate.  It does not cover expenditures
of the sort set forth in your letter, such as routine expenditures
made by an independent production company hired by an advertising
agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for
which a script has been written by the advertising agency.
Thus, the fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction
of special equipment costs, catering expenses, board and lodging expenses
incurred by the production company need not be separately reported
as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the
flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency.

     Turning to Mr. Hodgson's letter, I should first point out
that neither the proceeding against the Quentin Kopp Committee
nor the one against Butcher-Forde seems relevant to the questions
posed.  In particular, the characterization of the FPPC position
in these cases is not accurate.

     In the Kopp case, the treasurer was not named as a respondent.
In the Butcher-Forde suit, the Commission is not seeking civil
penalties for unreported payments of which the committee was not
aware.  A treasurer has an obligation to use reasonable diligence

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
September 12, 1980
Page Two


in obtaining and reporting under Sections 84303 and 84210(h),
and the vendor also has an obligation to provide the treasurer
with the necessary information under Section 84303. Beyond
that, neither the Political Reform Act nor the regulations specify
the means the treasurer must employ to insure that he has obtained
the necessary information from vendors.

Mr. Hodgson's first question concerned disclosure of "pass
through" payments and commissions. It was not clear exactly
what was meant by that question. If Mr. Hodgson has some specific
fact situations about which he wants advice, we can provide an
answer. However, I am afraid we are unable to answer this question
in its present, quite general form.

Mr. Hodgson's second question concerned disclosure of subcon-
tracting done by a vendor on his own initiative in the regular
course of the vendor's business. While the answer to this question
will depend in part on the nature of the work being subcontracted
by the vendor, the specific items mentioned by Mr. Hodgson such
as layout, printing, or delivery of a final product from the
printer to the purchaser would generally not be reportable under
Section 84303. If a specific factual situation arises where you
have a question as to reportability under Section 84303, please
feel free to request our advice again.

The answer to Mr. Hodgson's third question, concerning
nonpolitical personnel specifically hired by a vendor, also
depends on the specific factual situation. In the example given
of a painter who hires an assistant, Section 84303 would not
require reporting of the assistant's salary. The answer might
be different if a different type of personnel were hired, as,
for instance, if an advertising agency hired an independent
expert consultant to prepare a report on election trends and
develop a plan for a political advertising campaign.

I hope that this helps to clarify the reporting duties
imposed on treasurers by Sections 84303 and 84210(h). However,
since it is the specific facts of a situation which may deter-
minate, it is difficult to give conclusive advice on the basis
of general questions such as those posed by Mr. Hodgson.

If you have any further questions on this matter, I will be
happy to discuss them.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milfman
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

BAM:plh

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 550, 1228 EIGHTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

(916) 446-0752

June 18, 1980


Thomas M. Sobel, Esquire
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

Re: Earlier letters of October 25, 1979 and
    March 10, 1980.

Dear Mr. Sobel:

     I am enclosing copies of my letters of October 25, 1979 and March 10, 1980 to your office regarding our continued requests for clarification on the responsibilities of the campaign treasurer in light of Government Code § 84210(h), Government Code § 84303 and 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18427. I again raise these issues and ask for clarification since this particular area of campaign reporting has proved to be one of the most difficult to comply with since your office has given us virtually no guidelines in terms of what should or should not be reported. These issues were raised in October of 1979 at least six months prior to the initial campaign reports filed in conjunction with the Primary Election of 1980. The purpose of the letters of October, 1979, and March 1980 was to attempt to get some information from your office prior to the time when campaign reports are due during the election period. I personally can not think of a more important area that needs to be clarified in the campaign reporting area and would ask your office to give this letter immediate attention so there is some clear guidelines on the reporting issues that have been raised in my earlier letters to your office.

     Your courtesy and cooperation is appreciated. Please call me if you have any questions.

Very sincerely,

[signature]
JOHN H. HODGSON II

JHH:laj

Enclosures (2)


SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE • SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BLDG., ONE MARITIME PLAZA, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111 • (415) 362-1940
LOS ANGELES OFFICE • SUITE 309, 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, LOS ANGELES, CA 90017 • (213) 628-5867

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

FPPC

MAR 18 9 03 AM '80

March 15, 1980


Mr. Ted Prim
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Advice Request re Government Code Section 84210(h)

Dear Ted:

     We would appreciate clarification of the reporting
requirements for expenditures made to companies that produce
radio or television spots.

     A campaign manager usually presents the committee
with a proposed budget and an advertising plan. Most managers
then write the scripts, have them approved by the client and
then either produce the spots themselves or contract with a
production company. If the advertising firm produces these
spots, any payments made by the advertising firm for which
reimbursement is sought from the campaign would be disclosed
pursuant to Government Code Section 84303. However, if the
advertising firm contracts the production to an independent
production company for a flat fee which is paid by the cam-
paign committee, what disclosure is required?

     The expenditures usually made by the production
company are fees to actors and actresses, costs incurred at
the site of the production including rental charges, construc-
tion charges, special equipment, room and board, and many
related incidentals such as taxis, lunches, coffee, etc. In
the examples given to us, all these decisions and costs are
the responsibility of the production company and its profit
is the amount remaining once all costs are paid. I have
been informed that the usual practice is for the production
company personnel, and not the campaign, to choose the actors,
actresses, photographers, sound track technicians, etc..

     During the last few months, we have encountered
great resistance from two separate production companies when

SACRAMENTO OFFICE · SUITE 550, 1225 EIGHTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 · (916) 446-6752
LOS ANGELES OFFICE · SUITE 309, 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, LOS ANGELES, CA 90017 · (213) 628-5867

Mr. Ted Prim
March 15, 1980
Page 2


we instructed them to disclose to the campaign their expendi-
tures of $100 or more in producing the campaign spots. The
companies and the campaign managers argue that these companies
are not political organizations and are producing a product
for a fixed fee which is sufficient and complete disclosure.
They argue that no disclosure is required of the component
costs of other products that are purchased by campaigns such
as polling, printing, telephones, legal, accounting, etc..

Your assistance and cooperation is sincerely
appreciated.

Very truly yours,

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:rgs

. t.

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law
SUITE 250, 1225 EIGHTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

(916) 446-6752


March 10, 1980


Thomas M. Sobel, Esq.
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

Re:  Responsibilities of Campaign Treasurers

Dear Mr. Sobel:

Thank you for your letter of December 14, 1979 in
reply to my correspondence of October 25, 1979. The purpose of
my letter was to seek better clarification of the duties of a
committee treasurer. The motivation for this request is concern that Regulation 18427, including the Comment, does not
seem to provide treasurers with clear and adequate notice of
their responsibilities, particularly in light of the recent
FPPC lawsuit against a number of committees (and their campaign
consultant Butcher/Ford) and also, the recent FPPC enforcement
matter against the Quentin Kopp Committee. In the lawsuit, the
FPPC informs that court that treasurers should know how to
properly disclose expenditures containing commissions and
public relations fees, even though Regulation 18427 focuses
almost totally on contributions and your Campaign Disclosure
Manual is virtually silent on this subject. In the Kopp case,
the FPPC staff is questioning the diligence of a treasurer even
though the treasurer did question the source of the contribution,
did ask the candidate to obtain a transmittal letter from the
supposed donor to confirm his identity, and did receive the
confirming letter.

The question raised is how far past the invoice must
the treasurer look before paying a bill and what further itemization is required on the campaign report other than the payee.
For example, 1) must a treasurer disclose only "pass through"
payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's
trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no
benefit to any political persons? 2) Must a treasurer disclose
subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation
in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcontracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice
(i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design,


SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE • SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BLDG., ONE MARITIME PLAZA, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111 • (415) 362-1940
LOS ANGELES OFFICE • SUITE 309, 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, LOS ANGELES, CA 90017 • (213) 628-5867

Thomas M. Sobel, Esq.
Re: Responsibilities of Campaign Treasurers
March 10, 1980
Page Two

layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is
disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically
hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e.,
a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the
printing job or a CPA hires an additional bookkeeper during an
election year to help on various campaign accounts)? There are
a number of other variations of these situations which are
raised by Government Code §84210(h) and §84303.

It is clear that the FPPC believes that certain
payments by the payees are disclosable (payments by advertising
firms to the media, etc.), but the FPPC has given virtually no
clarification of its view on what other component payments are
disclosable.

Further, the Comments to Regulation 18427 are primarily
oriented to the treasurer's responsibility to question "suspect"
contribution data. What requirements are imposed on the treasurer
as it relates to expenditures? Does a treasurer need to require
an affidavit by all vendors to accompany each invoice stating
that the vendor understand the requirements of Government Code
§84210(h) and §84303 and has not made any such payments as part
of the invoice being submitted?

As mentioned in my October 25, 1979 letter, our
office would be please to work with you and your staff to
further explain the current confusions, as well as to work
toward a clarifying regulation or opinion.

Very sincerely,


JOHN H. HODGSON II

JHH:laj

bcc: VGN

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 550, 1225 EIGHTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

(916) 446-6752


October 25, 1979


FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attn: Ted Prim, Executive Director

Dear Mr. Prim:

As you are aware, this office represents a number of
political committees as well as serving as treasurer for other
committees. Because of the recent actions by the Fair Political Practices Commission, we are writing this letter to seek
clarification of the reporting responsibilities under Government Code §84303 and §84210(h). It is unclear what the
responsibilities of a treasurer are, particularly as they relate
to the reporting of expenditures under these two sections of the
law. I would ask that this matter be given immediate attention
because of the confusion that exists.

Our office would be pleased to work with your staff
to explain the current confusion as well as work towards a
clarifying regulation or opinion.

Very sincerely,


JOHN H. HODGSON II

JHH:laj

bcc: Chip
     Dana


SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE : SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BLDG., ONE MARITIME PLAZA, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111 · (415) 362-1840

sections.question: 1) Must a treasurer disclose only "pass through" payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no benefit to any political persons? 2) Must a treasurer disclose subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcontracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice (i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design, layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e., a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the printing job or a vendor hires an additional bookkeeper during an election year to help on various campaign accounts)?
sections.conclusion: Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement, or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report on election trends for a candidate. It does not cover expenditures such as routine expenditures made by an entertainment production company hired by an advertising agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for which a script has been written by the advertising agency. The fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction of special equipment costs, room, board, and incidental expenses incurred by the production company need not be separately reported as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency. Regarding pass-through payments and commissions, the question was too general to answer without specific fact situations. Regarding subcontracting done by a vendor on its own initiative, items such as layout, printing, or delivery of a final product from the printer would generally not be reportable under Section 84303. Regarding nonpolitical personnel hired by a vendor, such as a painter hiring an assistant, Section 84303 would not require reporting of the assistant's salary, though the answer might differ if an advertising agency hired an independent expert consultant.
sections.question_synthetic: What campaign treasurer reporting obligations exist under Government Code Section 84303 regarding vendor expenditures, including pass-through payments, subcontracting, and hiring of nonpolitical personnel by production companies and other vendors?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC concludes that Section 84303 requires reporting of the flat fee paid to production companies by advertising agencies, but not the component costs incurred by the production company itself (such as actor fees, equipment rental, or incidentals). Pass-through payments, vendor subcontracting in the regular course of business, and nonpolitical personnel hired by vendors generally do not require separate reporting, though the answer depends on specific factual situations involving the nature of the work and types of personnel involved.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple incoming letters from attorneys (March 15, 1980 from Vigo Nielsen regarding production company costs; March 10, 1980 from H. Hodgson regarding treasurer responsibilities; June 1980 follow-up from H. Hodgson) followed by FPPC response letter dated September 12, 1980 from Barbara A. Miliian, Staff Counsel. Some OCR degradation in original document affects readability of certain sections. Multiple related correspondence included showing chronology of requests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Must a treasurer disclose only "pass through" payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no benefit to any political persons? 2) Must a treasurer disclose subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcontracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice (i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design, layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e., a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the printing job or a vendor hires an additional bookkeeper during an election year to help on various campaign accounts)?

CONCLUSION: Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement, or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report on election trends for a candidate. It does not cover expenditures such as routine expenditures made by an entertainment production company hired by an advertising agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for which a script has been written by the advertising agency. The fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction of special equipment costs, room, board, and incidental expenses incurred by the production company need not be separately reported as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency. Regarding pass-through payments and commissions, the question was too general to answer without specific fact situations. Regarding subcontracting done by a vendor on its own initiative, items such as layout, printing, or delivery of a final product from the printer would generally not be reportable under Section 84303. Regarding nonpolitical personnel hired by a vendor, such as a painter hiring an assistant, Section 84303 would not require reporting of the assistant's salary, though the answer might differ if an advertising agency hired an independent expert consultant.

Nielsen, Jr. Dobbs & Nielsen 1225 Eighth Street, Suite 550 Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Mr. Nielsen: I have been asked to respond to your letter of March 15, 1980, to Ted Prim, and to Mr. Hodgson's letters of March 10, and June 16, 1980, to Tom Sobel. In your March 15 letter, you re- quested advice regarding reporting obligations under Government Code Section 84303 for expenditures to companies that produce radio or television spots. You described a factual situation in which an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee writes a script for such a spot advertisement, and an independent production company is hired by the agency for a flat fee to produce the advertisement. Section 84303 covers certain types of expenditures, such as payments by an advertising agency hired by a candidate's committee to a newspaper or television station for a political advertisement, or for an independent expert consultant hired to prepare a report on election trends for a candidate. It does not cover expenditures of the sort set forth in your letter, such as routine expenditures made by an independent production company hired by an advertising agency in the course of producing a political advertisement for which a script has been written by the advertising agency. Thus, the fees to actors and actresses, rental fees, construction of special equipment costs, catering expenses, board and lodging expenses incurred by the production company need not be separately reported as a result of Section 84303; it is sufficient to report the flat fee paid to the production company by the advertising agency. Turning to Mr. Hodgson's letter, I should first point out that neither the proceeding against the Quentin Kopp Committee nor the one against Butcher-Forde seems relevant to the questions posed. In particular, the characterization of the FPPC position in these cases is not accurate. In the Kopp case, the treasurer was not named as a respondent. In the Butcher-Forde suit, the Commission is not seeking civil penalties for unreported payments of which the committee was not aware. A treasurer has an obligation to use reasonable diligence Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr. September 12, 1980 Page Two in obtaining and reporting under Sections 84303 and 84210(h), and the vendor also has an obligation to provide the treasurer with the necessary information under Section 84303. Beyond that, neither the Political Reform Act nor the regulations specify the means the treasurer must employ to insure that he has obtained the necessary information from vendors. Mr. Hodgson's first question concerned disclosure of "pass through" payments and commissions. It was not clear exactly what was meant by that question. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to multiple questions about campaign treasurer reporting obligations under Government Code Section 84303, addressing the disclosure requirements for expenditures to production companies producing radio/television spots, pass-through payments, vendor subcontracting, and hiring of nonpolitical personnel.
